sections.title: 🧒 The Story
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
You type: www.myapp.com
Your browser needs to know: what IP address is that?

Without DNS: you'd need to remember 54.210.167.23 for every website.
With DNS: type a name → DNS translates → gets the IP → connects.

ROUTE 53 = AWS's DNS service
Named after: Route 66 (famous US highway) + port 53 (DNS port)

Route 53 does more than just translate names:
→ It can check if your servers are healthy
→ Route traffic to nearest server (lowest latency)
→ Automatically failover if a server goes down
→ Split traffic between versions (A/B testing)
→ Route users in Europe to EU servers, US users to US servers
```

---

blocks.content:
You type: www.myapp.com
Your browser needs to know: what IP address is that?

Without DNS: you'd need to remember 54.210.167.23 for every website.
With DNS: type a name → DNS translates → gets the IP → connects.

ROUTE 53 = AWS's DNS service
Named after: Route 66 (famous US highway) + port 53 (DNS port)

Route 53 does more than just translate names:
→ It can check if your servers are healthy
→ Route traffic to nearest server (lowest latency)
→ Automatically failover if a server goes down
→ Split traffic between versions (A/B testing)
→ Route users in Europe to EU servers, US users to US servers

sections.title: 📋 DNS Record Types
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
A RECORD:
→ Maps domain → IPv4 address
→ example.com → 54.210.167.23
→ www.example.com → 54.210.167.23
→ Most common record type

AAAA RECORD:
→ Maps domain → IPv6 address
→ example.com → 2001:0db8:85a3::8a2e:0370:7334

CNAME RECORD:
→ Maps domain → another domain name (alias)
→ www.example.com → example.com (then resolved again)
→ CANNOT be used on root domain (example.com → must use ALIAS instead)
→ Every CNAME lookup = extra DNS query (slight overhead)

ALIAS RECORD (AWS-specific):
→ Maps domain → AWS resource (ALB, CloudFront, S3 website, etc.)
→ Works on root domain! (example.com → ALB)
→ FREE (no charge for Alias queries to AWS resources)
→ AWS automatically follows resource IP changes
→ PREFER Alias over CNAME for AWS resources

NS RECORD:
→ Name Server — where to find DNS records for this domain
→ Route 53 gives you 4 NS records for each hosted zone
→ Register these with your domain registrar

MX RECORD:
→ Mail Exchange — where to send email for this domain
→ Priority: 10 mymail.example.com (lower number = higher priority)

TXT RECORD:
→ Text data — used for domain verification, SPF, DKIM
→ Google/AWS verify domain ownership via TXT records

SOA RECORD:
→ Start of Authority — admin info about the zone
→ Auto-created by Route 53

TTL (Time To Live):
→ How long DNS resolvers cache the record (in seconds)
→ Low TTL (60s): changes propagate fast, but more DNS queries (cost)
→ High TTL (86400s/24hr): fewer queries but slow to update
→ BEFORE making changes: lower TTL to 60s, make change, raise TTL back
→ Route 53 propagates globally in < 60 seconds
```

---

A RECORD:
→ Maps domain → IPv4 address
→ example.com → 54.210.167.23
→ www.example.com → 54.210.167.23

→ CANNOT be used on root domain (example.com → must use ALIAS instead)
→ Every CNAME lookup = extra DNS query (slight overhead)

ALIAS RECORD (AWS-specific):
→ Maps domain → AWS resource (ALB, CloudFront, S3 website, etc.)
→ Works on root domain! (example.com → ALB)
→ FREE (no charge for Alias queries to AWS resources)
→ AWS automatically follows resource IP changes
→ PREFER Alias over CNAME for AWS resources

NS RECORD:

→ Route 53 gives you 4 NS records for each hosted zone
→ Register these with your domain registrar

→ Mail Exchange — where to send email for this domain
→ Priority: 10 mymail.example.com (lower number = higher priority)

TXT RECORD:

→ Google/AWS verify domain ownership via TXT records

SOA RECORD:
→ Start of Authority — admin info about the zone
→ Auto-created by Route 53

TTL (Time To Live):
→ How long DNS resolvers cache the record (in seconds)
→ Low TTL (60s): changes propagate fast, but more DNS queries (cost)
→ High TTL (86400s/24hr): fewer queries but slow to update
→ BEFORE making changes: lower TTL to 60s, make change, raise TTL back
→ Route 53 propagates globally in < 60 seconds

revisionPoints: → Maps domain → IPv4 address
→ www.example.com → 54.210.167.23
→ Most common record type
→ Maps domain → IPv6 address
→ Maps domain → another domain name (alias)
→ www.example.com → example.com (then resolved again)

sections.title: 🗂️ Hosted Zones
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
HOSTED ZONE = container for DNS records for ONE domain

PUBLIC HOSTED ZONE:
→ Records visible to internet
→ Used for: your.com, api.your.com, www.your.com
→ Cost: $0.50/month per hosted zone

PRIVATE HOSTED ZONE:
→ Records visible ONLY within specific VPC(s)
→ Used for: internal.mycompany.com, db.internal (private DNS names)
→ EC2 instances use VPC's DNS resolver → finds private records
→ Cost: $0.50/month per hosted zone
→ Must enable: enableDnsHostnames + enableDnsSupport in VPC

EXAMPLE — PRIVATE HOSTED ZONE:
Zone: internal.myapp.com
Records:
  db.internal.myapp.com     → 10.0.21.15 (RDS private IP)
  cache.internal.myapp.com  → 10.0.21.30 (ElastiCache private IP)
  api.internal.myapp.com    → 10.0.11.0/24 (private app servers)

→ From EC2 inside VPC: ping db.internal.myapp.com → resolves
→ From internet: db.internal.myapp.com → does NOT resolve (private)
```

---

sections.title: 🌍 Routing Policies — The Core of Route 53
sections.rawMarkdown:
### 1. Simple Routing
```
One record → one or more IP values
→ Multiple IPs: Route 53 returns ALL, client picks one randomly
→ No health checks (returns even if server is down)
→ Use for: single resource, simple setups

example.com → [54.1.1.1, 54.1.1.2]
```

### 2. Weighted Routing
```
Route % of traffic to different resources
→ Total weight doesn't need to equal 100 (it's proportional)
→ With health checks: unhealthy record gets 0% traffic
→ Use for: A/B testing, blue-green deployments, gradual migration

Record 1: example.com → 54.1.1.1  Weight: 80
Record 2: example.com → 54.2.2.2  Weight: 20
→ 80% of traffic → server 1, 20% → server 2

MIGRATION EXAMPLE:
Week 1: old=90, new=10  (canary test)
Week 2: old=50, new=50  (split test)
Week 3: old=10, new=90  (mostly new)
Week 4: old=0,  new=100 (complete migration)
```

### 3. Latency-Based Routing
```
Route users to the region with LOWEST latency for them
→ AWS measures latency from user → each region
→ Routes to fastest region
→ With health checks: unhealthy regions excluded

Records:
example.com → ALB-us-east-1  Region: us-east-1
example.com → ALB-eu-west-1  Region: eu-west-1
example.com → ALB-ap-south-1 Region: ap-south-1

User in Mumbai → measured latency: US=180ms, EU=120ms, India=8ms
→ Routed to ap-south-1 (8ms wins!)

USE FOR: global applications where response time matters
```

### 4. Failover Routing
```
Active-Passive failover with health checks
→ PRIMARY: receives all traffic while healthy
→ SECONDARY: receives traffic only if primary is unhealthy

Records:
example.com → ALB-primary  Type: PRIMARY  Health Check: ✓
example.com → ALB-secondary Type: SECONDARY (no health check — always shown as backup)

Health Check fails on primary → Route 53 automatically routes to secondary
Health Check passes again → traffic returns to primary

USE FOR: disaster recovery, high availability
DR STRATEGY WITH ROUTE 53:
Primary region fails → Route 53 detects unhealthy → switches DNS to DR region
Failover time: ~60 seconds (health check interval + TTL)
```

### 5. Geolocation Routing
```
Route based on USER'S GEOGRAPHIC LOCATION
→ NOT latency (that's latency-based) — this is purely geography
→ Country, continent, or US state level
→ Must have a default record (catch-all for unknown locations)

Records:
example.com → EU-servers    Location: Europe
example.com → US-servers    Location: United States
example.com → APAC-servers  Location: Asia Pacific
example.com → US-servers    Location: Default (catch-all)

USE CASES:
→ Compliance: EU users MUST be served from EU (GDPR data residency)
→ Language: Japanese users → Japanese content
→ Legal: some content only licensed for specific countries
→ NOT for performance (use latency-based for performance)
```

### 6. Geoproximity Routing (Traffic Flow)
```
Route based on geographic location of users AND resources
→ Can BIAS (shift) traffic toward a region artificially
→ Bias +1 to +99: attract MORE traffic to that region
→ Bias -1 to -99: push traffic AWAY from that region
→ Requires Route 53 Traffic Flow (visual editor, $50/month/policy)

USE FOR: fine-tuned global traffic distribution
```

### 7. Multi-Value Answer Routing
```
Like Simple but WITH health checks
→ Returns up to 8 healthy records randomly
→ Client picks one (client-side load balancing)
→ Unhealthy → excluded from responses
→ NOT a substitute for a real load balancer (no stickiness, no advanced features)
→ Good for: simple client-side load balancing with health awareness
```

### 8. IP-Based Routing
```
Route based on CLIENT'S IP ADDRESS (not geography)
→ Useful when you know your users' IP CIDR ranges
→ Example: corporate users (specific IP range) → internal version
→ Internet users (all other IPs) → public version
→ ISP optimization: route certain ISPs to specific endpoints
```

---

sections.title: 💚 Health Checks
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
Route 53 health checks = monitors endpoint health, used by routing policies

TYPES:
1. Endpoint Health Check:
   → HTTP/HTTPS/TCP check to specific endpoint
   → Checks from 15+ global Route 53 locations
   → Threshold: 3/5 health checkers must agree it's unhealthy
   → Interval: 30 seconds (standard) or 10 seconds (fast, extra cost)
   → Expected response: 2xx/3xx status codes (or specific string match)

2. Calculated Health Check:
   → Combines multiple health checks with AND/OR logic
   → "Healthy if 2 out of 3 checks pass"
   → Use for: complex health determination

3. CloudWatch Alarm Health Check:
   → Health = based on CloudWatch alarm state
   → Use for: private resources (Route 53 can't reach private VPC directly)
   → EC2 in private subnet → CloudWatch alarm → health check watches alarm

HEALTH CHECK → ROUTING POLICY INTEGRATION:
Failover: health check on primary → fails → Route 53 uses secondary
Weighted: health check on each record → unhealthy = 0% traffic
Latency: health check on each region → unhealthy region excluded
Multi-Value: health check on each → only healthy IPs returned

MONITORING:
→ Health checks publish to CloudWatch
→ Create CloudWatch Alarm on health check status
→ Alert on-call if health check fails
```

---

blocks.content:
Route 53 health checks = monitors endpoint health, used by routing policies

TYPES:
1. Endpoint Health Check:
   → HTTP/HTTPS/TCP check to specific endpoint
   → Checks from 15+ global Route 53 locations
   → Threshold: 3/5 health checkers must agree it's unhealthy
   → Interval: 30 seconds (standard) or 10 seconds (fast, extra cost)
   → Expected response: 2xx/3xx status codes (or specific string match)

2. Calculated Health Check:
   → Combines multiple health checks with AND/OR logic
   → "Healthy if 2 out of 3 checks pass"
   → Use for: complex health determination

3. CloudWatch Alarm Health Check:
   → Health = based on CloudWatch alarm state
   → Use for: private resources (Route 53 can't reach private VPC directly)
   → EC2 in private subnet → CloudWatch alarm → health check watches alarm

HEALTH CHECK → ROUTING POLICY INTEGRATION:
Failover: health check on primary → fails → Route 53 uses secondary
Weighted: health check on each record → unhealthy = 0% traffic
Latency: health check on each region → unhealthy region excluded
Multi-Value: health check on each → only healthy IPs returned

MONITORING:
→ Health checks publish to CloudWatch
→ Create CloudWatch Alarm on health check status
→ Alert on-call if health check fails

sections.title: 🎯 Interview Q&A — Route 53
sections.rawMarkdown:
**Q: What's the difference between CNAME and Alias records?**
```
"CNAME maps a name to another name, which then gets resolved again.
Limitation: cannot be used at the zone apex (root domain).
example.com CANNOT have a CNAME record (DNS spec prohibits it).
www.example.com can have CNAME → example.com. Fine.

Alias is AWS-specific. It maps a name directly to an AWS resource
(ALB, CloudFront distribution, S3 website endpoint, another Route 53 record).
Works at the zone apex! example.com → your ALB. This is what you need
for pointing your root domain at an AWS load balancer.

Also: Alias queries to AWS resources are free. Route 53 doesn't charge
for DNS queries that resolve to AWS resources via Alias. CNAME queries
are charged normally.

In practice: always use Alias for AWS resources, CNAME for non-AWS."
```

**Q: How do you implement blue-green deployment with Route 53?**
```
"Two approaches depending on speed requirement:

Weighted routing approach (gradual):
Blue environment: example.com → blue-ALB, Weight: 100
Green environment: example.com → green-ALB, Weight: 0

Deploy to green. Test it.
Shift: blue=90, green=10 (canary — 10% gets new version)
Monitor error rates, latency for 30 minutes.
Shift: blue=0, green=100 (complete cutover)
If problems: blue=100, green=0 (instant rollback)
TTL: set to 60 seconds so changes propagate in 1 minute.

Failover routing approach (instant):
Primary: example.com → green-ALB (active, health check enabled)
Secondary: example.com → blue-ALB (standby)

Deploy to secondary. Test it. Promote secondary to primary.
For true instant switchover: 
Just update the primary record to point to green. Takes < 60 seconds
with low TTL. But requires DNS to propagate (clients may cache old TTL).

For zero-propagation-delay switching: do it at the ALB level
(weighted target groups) not DNS level."
```

---

for pointing your root domain at an AWS load balancer.

Also: Alias queries to AWS resources are free. Route 53 doesn't charge
for DNS queries that resolve to AWS resources via Alias. CNAME queries

In practice: always use Alias for AWS resources, CNAME for non-AWS."

Deploy to secondary. Test it. Promote secondary to primary.
For true instant switchover: 
Just update the primary record to point to green. Takes < 60 seconds
with low TTL. But requires DNS to propagate (clients may cache old TTL).

For zero-propagation-delay switching: do it at the ALB level
(weighted target groups) not DNS level."

sections.title: 🔗 Connects To
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
Route 53 ← Domain Registration: buy domains directly in Route 53
Route 53 → ALB: most common A record target (Alias)
Route 53 → CloudFront: CDN endpoint (Alias)
Route 53 → S3 Website: static sites (Alias)
Route 53 → EC2 Elastic IP: direct IP (A record)
Route 53 → Health Checks → CloudWatch: alerting on endpoint health
Route 53 ← Traffic Flow: visual routing policy editor

NEXT: CloudFront — CDN
How to serve your content globally with low latency, and how
CloudFront integrates with S3, ALB, and Route 53.
```

---

How to serve your content globally with low latency, and how
CloudFront integrates with S3, ALB, and Route 53.
